FROM python:3.11
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "main.py"]
Details:
{
  "Answer": {
    "Title": "Dockerfile Layer Caching Optimization",
    "Explanation": [
      "The problem is that `COPY . /app` copies all source files before running `pip install`. Since Docker invalidates all layers after a changed layer, any source code modification invalidates the COPY layer and forces `pip install` to re-run.",
      "",
      "The fix leverages Docker's layer caching by splitting the COPY into two steps:",
      "1. First, set the WORKDIR.",
      "2. Copy only `requirements.txt` into the container.",
      "3. Run `pip install` — this layer is now only invalidated when `requirements.txt` itself changes.",
      "4. Then copy the rest of the application source code.",
      "",
      "This way, if only source code files (like `main.py`) change but `requirements.txt` remains the same, Docker reuses the cached layer for `pip install` and only re-runs the second `COPY . .` and subsequent layers, dramatically speeding up rebuilds."

FROM python:3.11
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "main.py"]
The pipe causes the `while` loop to execute in a subshell, so increments to `count` are lost when the subshell exits; the parent shell's `count` remains 0.

This causes pip install to re-run unnecessarily.",

      "The solution leverages Docker's layer caching by separating concerns: we copy only the requirements.txt file first and run pip install, then copy the remaining source code.
